system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.
user_prompt:
Chapter 9: Jazz Standard Study #8: Have You Met Miss Jones
Pages 133-148 of the book.

<<<CHAPTER TEXT

| want to warn you, the next several songs in our jazz standards stud-
ies are by far the most harmonically complex. Perhaps not the most
difficult to understand, but they certainly have the most non-diatonic
key center changes.

Have You Met Miss Jones is the standard that will kick them off. This
iS A great tune and another one that is important to have in your rep-
ertoire. But Miss Jones has a lot to teach us about harmony and the
way other jazz standards operate. I’m excited to dive into this one, and

| hope you are too!
Have You Met Miss Jenes tHe

Have You Met Miss Jones was written by Richard Rodgers with lyrics by Lorenz
Hart in 1937 for the musical comedy “I'd Rather Be Right.”

The bridge utilizes three different key changes in intervals of 3rds which is said
to have served as an inspiration for John Coltrane’s development of “Coltrane
Changes.”

It is most commonly played in the key of Concert F major.

Have You Met Miss Jones does have a shifting bridge which we will
talk more about in the chords analysis. It makes sense that this song
may have inspired Uohn Coltrane because songs like “Giant Steps”
follow this exact formula.


Here are some recordings to check out.

>> SUGGESTED LISTENING

Frank Sinatra from “Swing Along with Me” 1961

Louis Armstrong from “Under the Stars” 1957

Oscar Peterson from “We Get Requests” 1964

Stan Getz from “Quintets: The Clef and Norgran
Studio Albums” 2011 (Compilation)

As are most of these jazz standards, Have You Met Miss Jones is
another one worth trying to learn the lyrics to, regardless of whether
you are a vocalist or not. Hence why | have included Frank Sinatra and
Louis Armstrong versions above.

Of course, there are many other versions of this song to listen to. This
is just scratching the surface. In general, | have included older record-
ings in this book, but | personally think it’s really fun to listen to how
modern players approach these standards as well.

Okay, let’s take a look at the chord map, guide tone chart, and chords
analysis.

For this chords analysis, | have color coded the sections according to
key centers. When appropriate, the roman numerals relate back to the
parent key.

‘Blue = F major (parent key)

That's four different key centers we are dealing with. That’s a decent
amount of mileage to go through on Have You Met Miss Jones, but
don’t let it intimidate you too much.

The first 8 bars of the form are blue, meaning they are diatonic with
the parent key center. This is diatonic harmony 101. But let’s take a
look at that first line where something calls for our attention.

Fai? Fon” Guin’
I #idim ii
(Sub for VI7)

Take a good look at the F#dim7. At first glance, this may seem un-
related to the key center, but in reality, it fits in almost perfectly. The
F#tdim/7 chord is a substitute for the dominant VI7 chord.

Since we know that dominant 7 chords can have extensions added to
them (9ths, Tlths, and 13ths) with different alterations (b9, #11, b13
etc...), we can easily make the vi chord into a VI7b9 chord. Jazz musi-
cians will often add different altered extensions to chords to add more
tension and resolution to chord progressions.

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK far |



Let’s assume for a second that the dominant VI7 chord has a b9 add-
ed to it. Therefore, it would be a D7(b9). Since I’m telling you that the
F#tdim7 is being substituted for that chord, take a look at these two
chords side by side.

Fyn? pr
3b

F#dim7: F#-A-C-Eb
D7(b9): D-F#-A-C-Eb

Do you see the similarities in these two chords? They are virtually the
same except the root in the D7(b9). You can think of the F#dim7 as a
rootless D7(b9).

Now, on a compositional level the F#dim7 is functioning as more than
just a substitution. It’s also a creating a chromatic passing note, in this
case the bass note, to connect the | chord with the ii chord.

This is basic diminished theory here, and there is a lot more where that
came from. But for the purposes of this jazz standard study, we are
going to stop here. | explain this so that you understand why the dimin-
ished passing chord is there and how it works.

Now let’s check out the bridge which is what makes this song so har-
monically important and unique.


[4 [ 2
Guin” C7 Cun” F’

ae | ii-V-I in Gb major - - - -- ------------- ii-V-I in D major - - -

Daa’ Aun” p” GPa? Gwin’ Cc’

- ene ee nee ee--e- ii-V-I in Gb major - - - -- -------------- ii-V of Tonic

Starting at the 2" ending, the first key change starts. In this case it’s
to the key of Bb major which is the IV chord of the parent key. Bars
9-11 are a simple ii-V-| progression.

This is where things get weird. The Bomaj7 in bar 11 transitions down a
whole step to an Abmin7 in bar 12. This starts a short ii-V-| chord pro-
gression into the key of Gb major.

After that, there isn’t much time to breathe. The Gbmaj7 transitions
down a whole step to an Emin7 in bar 14, which starts a short ii-V-I into
the key of D major. The following bars utilize another ii-V-| into Gb ma-
jor. The Gbmaj7 in bar 17 moves up a half step to a Gmin7 in bar 18,
which begins a ii-V back into the parent key tonic.

Phew!

There are a lot of key changes going on and happening quite quickly.
No doubt, it can be tough to navigate these chord changes.

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK 143 |



Luckily, when we break this bridge section down into key centers, it
begins to feel easier to understand. Instead of looking at it as a mish-
mash of seemingly unrelated chords, you can just look at it as a bunch
of ii-V-l’s in different key centers.

| know I’ve said this a lot, but I'll say it again: if you know your ii-V-l’s
you are at a huge advantage. That’s why | started this book off with
Autumn Leaves. | wanted you to understand the basics of how major
and minor ii-V-l’s work.

But what exactly is going on here? Is there any rhyme or reason to why
Richard Rodgers wrote the bridge this way? To our ear this sounds
good. Why is that?

The first element that gives the bridge so much strength is the
melody. I’ve already talked about how important the melody is to any
song, so this shouldn’t come as a surprise. If you listen to how the
melody is composed over these chord changes, it’s perfect. It con-
nects smoothly between the different key centers so that despite
the cycle of non-diatonic key centers, it sounds completely natural.

The second element is the pattern being used. Yes, it’s a series of
ii-V-l’s which our ears recognize. But the key centers are moving in a
particular order. They are moving in 3rds.

Bb to Gb is a descending major 3" interval.
Gb to D is a descending major 3" interval.
D to Gb is an ascending major 3" interval.

So, there is some order to the madness. This pattern helps our ears
latch on to the movement.


IMPROV LESSON
Duninshed Licks ana Navigating the
iB Section

For this Improv Lesson | want to give you some musical ideas to play
over the form of Have You Met Miss Jones. As discussed in the chords
analysis, the most challenging aspects of this tune are the passing
diminished chord in the A section and the entire B section.

Let’s start with addressing the passing diminished chord. How do we
improvise over that?

Remember that it is being substituted for the VI7b9 chord, so any of
those chord tones will serve you well. To get warmed up, here’s a chord
tone exercise over the first 4 bars repeated. This is similar to ones we
have done in other studies.

For total clarity, I’ve labeled all of the chord tones. But as far as other
note choices go for the diminished 7 chord outside of the basic chord
tones, take a look at this scale.

THE WHOLE-HALF DIMINISHED SCALE

Recorded 15 ma

BoE im in P

I’ve listed the scale tones beneath the notes to help you understand
the scale formula. It’s called the “Whole-Half” diminished because
the intervallic formula for this scale is: W-H-W-H-W-H-W. The scale is
based on alternating whole steps and half steps.

| show this to you so that you know the possible note choices over a di-
minished 7 chord. Of course, | don’t want you to play this scale verba-
tim when you are improvising. That’s why | think the best way to help
you navigate these changes is to give you some musical ideas as a
reference. In my experience, just playing the music rather than spend-
ing too much time getting bogged down in theory is more helpful.

Here are a few licks | want you to try over the first 4 bars of the A sec-
tion.

center by half steps or whole steps. I’m referring to the first and last

but it does allow for a smooth flow in the lines.

It's also perfectly fine to not connected the key centers together. |
did this in bar 3 of this last lick. | ended on a half note and started a
new line in the next bar. Nothing wrong with that. As long as your line
doesn’t sound like it’s unnaturally starting and stopping, this can be a
great solution.

Have You Met Miss Jones is an excellent study, and | hope you see
why | have included it in this book. Hopefully, some of the mystery of
the bridge has been unveiled for you.

Let’s move on to our second to last jazz standard study: All the Things
You Are.
